main_ingredients: mala paste (fermented broad beans, Sichuan peppercorns, dried chilies)
allergens: soy (fermented broad bean paste may contain soy)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Mala Xiang Guo (soup version) is approximately 400 g, representing a single-person main dish portion inclusive of mixed meats, vegetables, tofu, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Mala Xiang Guo (soup) exists in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB database entry for mixed stir-fried dishes with high oil and spice content, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) composite values for mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry for stir-fried mixed dish with chili oil base. Input record values (energy 185 kcal, protein 8.2 g, fat 14.5 g, carbohydrate 6.8 g, sodium 450 mg) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g based on chili oil and animal fat content typical of mala preparations per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mala Xiang Guo. GI of 42 is estimated by analogy to mixed stir-fried vegetable and meat dishes with high fat and fibre content, consistent with published GI values for similar composite dishes (GI range 35–50) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (42 × 27.2) / 100 = 11.4 g (carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 6.8 × 4 = 27.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; ~1800 mg per standard 400 g serving) from mala paste, fermented broad beans, and chili oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Mala Xiang Guo due to its very high sodium load (~1800 mg per serving, representing approximately 78% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg); if consumed, request reduced paste and sauce, and avoid adding soy sauce or additional salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 42) and low sugar content (0.8 g per 100 g) are favourable, but high fat content (14.5 g per 100 g) and variable carbohydrate load from optional starchy ingredients (potato, noodles, rice) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mala Xiang Guo can be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and low sugar content; however, starchy add-ins such as potato, noodles, or rice should be minimised, and the high fat content monitored to avoid excess caloric intake and postprandial lipid elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14.5 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat of ~4.5 g per 100 g from chili oil and animal fats, combined with very high sodium (~1800 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mala Xiang Guo only occasionally and in small portions, given its high saturated fat and sodium content, which together may elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; selecting leaner protein options (tofu, chicken) and requesting reduced oil and paste can partially mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin from chilies and piperine from Sichuan peppercorns may have anti-inflammatory properties, but high oil and sodium content may be pro-inflammatory if consumed excessively
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mala Xiang Guo may be consumed occasionally by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the capsaicin and piperine content may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit, but excessive oil and sodium intake should be avoided to prevent systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mala Xiang Guo (麻辣香锅), literally 'numbing-spicy fragrant pot', is a highly customisable Sichuan-style dish originating from Chongqing and Sichuan Province, China, that has become widely popular across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The dish is defined by its signature 'mala' flavour profile — 'ma' (麻, numbing) derived from Sichuan peppercorns and 'la' (辣, spicy) from dried chilies and chili oil. In its soup variant, the mala base is prepared as a richly spiced broth or sauce rather than a dry stir-fry. Diners select from a wide array of raw ingredients — thinly sliced meats (beef, pork, chicken), seafood, tofu products, mushrooms, and vegetables such as lotus root, broccoli, cabbage, and potato — which are then wok-fried or simmered in a potent mala paste made from fermented broad beans (doubanjiang), Sichuan peppercorns, dried chilies, ginger, garlic, and numerous aromatics. The result is a fragrant, oily, intensely flavoured dish with a numbing heat sensation. Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 740 kcal, 32.8 g protein, 58 g fat, and 27.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high (~1800 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 42 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood sugar management when starchy add-ins are limited. The dish is rich in iron, potassium, vitamin A, and vitamin C from its vegetable components. It is not suitable for low-sodium or low-fat diets.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mala Xiang Guo (soup) exists in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB mixed stir-fried dish composites and USDA SR Legacy stir-fry entries, which are consistent with the input record values. Saturated fat is estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for mixed high-fat, high-fibre stir-fried dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for mixed stir-fried dishes with chili oil and animal fat base; no direct measurement available for this specific preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Mala Xiang Guo (soup) are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of April 2026. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): Mala Xiang Guo contains no significant vitamin D source; this is consistent with HPB and USDA data for comparable mixed stir-fried dishes. 4. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mala Xiang Guo has been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 5. The dish is highly customisable; nutrient values represent a typical mixed preparation and will vary substantially depending on ingredient selection. 6. The input source 'Journal of Culinary Science & Technology / Tandfonline' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; values have been re-anchored to HPB and USDA SR Legacy. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as mala paste formulations vary by manufacturer and restaurant.
